questions.question: India's Five Year Plans were a feature of which economic model?
options: Socialist/Mixed economy planning — Soviet-influenced centralized planning to guide economic development (1951-2017)
Pure capitalist economy
Free market economy
Laissez-faire economy
questions.explanation: India's Five-Year Plans (1951-2017, 12 plans total) were inspired by Soviet Union's central planning model. First Plan (1951-56): agriculture focus (Bhakra dam, community development). Second Plan (Nehru-Mahalanobis): heavy industry. 12th Plan ended 2017. NITI Aayog replaced with strategy documents.

questions.question: The First Five Year Plan of India (1951-56) focused on:
options: Agriculture, irrigation, and power (dams: Bhakra-Nangal, Hirakud, Damodar Valley); based on Harrod-Domar growth model
Heavy industrialization
IT sector
Infrastructure only
questions.explanation: First FYP (1951-56): priority on agriculture, irrigation, and power generation to overcome food shortages and post-partition economic disruption. Planned outlay: Rs 2,069 crore. Major projects: Bhakra-Nangal (Punjab), Damodar Valley Corporation, Hirakud Dam. Also focused on land reforms.

questions.question: Public Distribution System (PDS) in India is meant to:
options: Distribute subsidized food grains (rice, wheat, sugar, kerosene) to Below Poverty Line (BPL) families through ration shops (Fair Price Shops)
Distribute fertilizers
Manage mid-day meals in schools
Export food grains
questions.explanation: PDS: network of about 5.3 lakh Fair Price Shops (ration shops) distributing subsidized food. Managed jointly by central (Food Corporation of India procures and allocates) and state governments (distributes). NFSA 2013 (National Food Security Act) extended PDS to ~81 crore people (75% rural, 50% urban population).

questions.question: The Minimum Support Price (MSP) is:
options: Government-guaranteed price for agricultural produce — ensures farmers get fair price and are not exploited by market forces; recommended by CACP
Market price of commodities
Minimum wage for agricultural workers
Tax on agricultural income
questions.explanation: MSP: announced by Central Government (based on CACP — Commission for Agricultural Costs and Prices recommendations) before sowing season. Government agencies (FCI, NAFED) purchase crops at MSP if market price falls below it. MSP for 23 major crops including paddy, wheat, pulses, oilseeds, and cotton. Not legally binding on private buyers.

questions.question: The National Rural Employment Guarantee Act (MGNREGA) guarantees:
options: 100 days of unskilled manual work per year to any rural household that demands it — minimum wage paid, unemployed allowance if work not provided within 15 days
200 days of work
Employment to urban workers
Skilled employment only
questions.explanation: MGNREGA (2005, implemented 2006): provides legal right to 100 days of unskilled work per household per year at minimum wage. If work not provided within 15 days, unemployment allowance paid. Works include road construction, water conservation, land development. Among world's largest social protection programs. Renamed to include Gandhi: MGNREGS.

questions.question: The Pradhan Mantri Jan Dhan Yojana (PMJDY) aims at:
options: Financial inclusion — opening zero-balance bank accounts for unbanked sections with RuPay debit card, overdraft facility, and insurance
Free LPG connections
Healthcare for poor
Rural electrification
questions.explanation: PMJDY (August 28, 2014): world's largest financial inclusion initiative. Features: zero-balance savings account (Jan Dhan account), RuPay debit card with Rs 2 lakh accident insurance, Rs 30,000 life insurance, Rs 10,000 overdraft facility. By 2024: 52+ crore accounts opened, mostly in rural areas.

questions.question: The NABARD (National Bank for Agriculture and Rural Development) was established in:
options: 1982 — apex bank for agricultural and rural credit; provides refinance to cooperative banks, RRBs, and commercial banks for agricultural loans
questions.explanation: NABARD (est. July 12, 1982) based on B. Sivaraman Committee recommendations. HQ: Mumbai. Functions: refinancing apex cooperative banks, RRBs (Regional Rural Banks), and commercial banks for agriculture/rural development; rural infrastructure development (RIDF); watershed development; Self-Help Group (SHG) - Bank Linkage Programme.

questions.question: The Pradhan Mantri Awas Yojana (PMAY) aims to provide:
options: Housing for All — affordable housing to urban and rural poor by 2022 (extended to 2024); includes PMAY-Urban and PMAY-Gramin
Free food for all
Employment guarantee
Healthcare for all
questions.explanation: PMAY (2015): targets housing for all urban slum dwellers and rural homeless. PMAY-Urban: credit-linked subsidy scheme for home loans (for EWS/LIG/MIG). PMAY-Gramin: provides Rs 1.2 lakh (plain areas) or Rs 1.3 lakh (NE, hilly) grant for rural house construction. Completed 3 crore+ houses.

questions.question: The term 'poverty line' in India is determined by:
options: Minimum monthly per capita expenditure needed to meet basic food, clothing, and shelter needs — currently based on Tendulkar Committee methodology (modified by Rangarajan Committee)
Annual income below Rs 1 lakh
BPL card possession
Urban/rural classification only
questions.explanation: India's Poverty Line: Tendulkar Committee (2009): Rs 816/month urban, Rs 672/month rural (2011-12 prices). Rangarajan Committee (2014): higher threshold — Rs 1407 urban, Rs 972 rural. SECC (Socio-Economic and Caste Census) used for targeting welfare programs. About 21% of population below poverty line (World Bank $1.90/day PPP criterion).

questions.question: Remittances from Non-Resident Indians (NRIs) contribute to India's:
options: Current Account (in Balance of Payments) — India is the world's largest remittance recipient (~$100 billion annually)
Capital Account only
Trade Balance (goods exports)
Fiscal deficit
questions.explanation: NRI remittances: India consistently receives the highest remittances globally (~$100 billion in 2022-23). Recorded under 'transfers' in the Current Account of BoP. Major sources: USA, UAE, Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, UK. They help finance India's current account deficit and support rural household incomes.

questions.question: The National Rural Health Mission (NHM) was launched in:
options: 2005 — to improve healthcare in rural India; later merged with National Urban Health Mission to form NHM in 2013; ASHAs (Accredited Social Health Activists) are key workers
questions.explanation: NRHM (National Rural Health Mission, 2005): flagship health scheme for rural areas. Key feature: ASHA workers — trained female community health activists (one per 1000 population) who link communities with health facilities. NRHM + National Urban Health Mission merged into NHM (National Health Mission) in 2013.

questions.question: The Atal Innovation Mission (AIM) was established under:
options: NITI Aayog — to promote innovation and entrepreneurship across India through Atal Tinkering Labs (ATLs) in schools and Atal Incubation Centres (AICs)
Ministry of Finance
CSIR
RBI
questions.explanation: AIM (2016, under NITI Aayog): flagship initiative to promote innovation. Key programs: Atal Tinkering Labs (ATLs) — maker spaces in schools (Grades 6-12) with 3D printers, robotics kits, etc. Atal Incubation Centres (AICs): support startups. Atal Community Innovation Centres (ACICs): tier-2/3 cities and rural areas.

questions.question: The Eleventh Schedule of the Indian Constitution lists subjects under:
options: Panchayati Raj institutions (29 subjects including agriculture, land, roads, drinking water, education, poverty alleviation) after 73rd Amendment 1992
State legislature subjects
Union List subjects
Concurrent List subjects
questions.explanation: 73rd Constitutional Amendment (1992): gave constitutional status to Panchayati Raj (3-tier local self-government). 11th Schedule: 29 subjects devolved to panchayats including: agriculture, land reforms, rural electrification, water management, primary education, primary health, roads, poverty alleviation. States choose which subjects to actually transfer.

questions.question: The Swachh Bharat Abhiyan (Clean India Mission) was launched on:
options: October 2, 2014 (Gandhi Jayanti) — to achieve Open Defecation Free (ODF) India; 9 crore+ toilets built; PM Modi started by personally sweeping Valmiki colony, New Delhi
August 15, 2014
January 26, 2015
April 5, 2016
questions.explanation: Swachh Bharat Mission (Oct 2, 2014, Gandhi's 145th birthday): two sub-missions — SBM-Grameen (rural, MoDRWS) and SBM-Urban (MoHUA). Phase 1 (2014-19): ODF (Open Defecation Free) goal — 60 crore people stopped open defecation. Phase 2 (2020-25): ODF+, solid/liquid waste management, plastic waste management.

questions.question: The Bharat Net project aims to:
options: Provide broadband connectivity to all Gram Panchayats (2.5 lakh panchayats) using OFC, radio, and satellite — run by BSNL/DOT; enable Digital India in rural areas
Build highways
Develop ports
Electrify villages (that's Saubhagya scheme)
questions.explanation: BharatNet (2011, earlier NOFN — National Optical Fibre Network): world's largest rural broadband connectivity project. Connects all 2.5 lakh gram panchayats with optical fiber (OFC). Implemented by BSNL through state governments. Enables: e-governance, telemedicine, e-education, e-commerce in rural India. Essential backbone for Digital India.

questions.question: Food Corporation of India (FCI) was established to:
options: Procure food grains from farmers at MSP, store, and distribute them through PDS — established 1965; HQ: New Delhi; India's largest food storage network
Export food grains
Process food products
Regulate food prices directly
questions.explanation: FCI (1965, FCI Act 1964): functions: procurement of wheat and rice from farmers at MSP (mainly Punjab, Haryana, UP), building and managing strategic food reserves (buffer stocks), distributing to state governments for PDS at Central Issue Price (CIP). FCI's buffer stock of grain helps stabilize prices and ensure food security.

questions.question: The Pradhan Mantri Kaushal Vikas Yojana (PMKVY) is a scheme for:
options: Skill development — free short-term training to youth in industry-relevant skills; recognition of prior learning (RPL); National Skill Development Corporation (NSDC) implements it
Mid-day meals
Employment guarantee
Housing for youth
questions.explanation: PMKVY (2015): flagship skill development scheme under Skill India Mission. Free short-term training across 300+ job roles in 38 sectors (construction, IT, healthcare, agriculture, etc.). On successful training + assessment: placement assistance and Rs 8,000 reward. PMKVY 4.0: focus on Industry 4.0 skills, digital and green jobs.

questions.question: The Bharat Broadband Network Limited (BBNL) was merged with BSNL to implement:
options: BharatNet project — providing optical fiber broadband to all gram panchayats; BBNL was Special Purpose Vehicle set up 2012; merged with BSNL 2023
4G rollout in cities
Satellite internet
National telecom policy
questions.explanation: BBNL (Bharat Broadband Network Limited, 2012): Special Purpose Vehicle to implement BharatNet (initially NOFN). In 2023, government merged BBNL with BSNL to streamline the BharatNet project and leverage BSNL's existing infrastructure, workforce, and right-of-way permissions for faster rural broadband rollout.

questions.question: The Asian Development Bank (ADB) is headquartered in:
options: Manila, Philippines — established 1966; provides loans/technical assistance to developing Asian countries; India is the largest borrower from ADB
Tokyo
Singapore
Beijing
questions.explanation: ADB (Asian Development Bank, 1966): HQ in Manila, Philippines. 68 member countries (49 from Asia-Pacific). Provides: project loans, policy-based loans, technical assistance, equity investments. India is one of ADB's largest borrowers (infrastructure, urban development, agriculture). USA and Japan are largest shareholders.

questions.question: The Pradhan Mantri Gram Sadak Yojana (PMGSY) was launched to:
options: Provide all-weather road connectivity to unconnected habitations (500+ population in plain areas, 250+ in hilly/tribal areas) — launched 2000, PM Atal Bihari Vajpayee
Build national highways
Build expressways
Develop urban roads only
questions.explanation: PMGSY (2000): flagship scheme to provide all-weather road connectivity to unconnected rural habitations. Target: 500+ population habitations in plain areas, 250+ in NE/hilly/tribal. World's largest rural road program. PMGSY Phase III (2019): upgrade 1.25 lakh km of existing rural roads. Funded 60% Centre: 40% State (90:10 for NE/special category).

questions.question: NABARD's function of refinancing is:
options: Providing funds to cooperative banks, Regional Rural Banks (RRBs), and commercial banks against their agriculture/rural lending portfolio — enabling them to extend more credit to farmers
Providing loans directly to farmers only
Setting agricultural prices
Managing FCI stocks
questions.explanation: NABARD refinancing: Short-term credit (seasonal agricultural operations: crop loans) and Long-term credit (land development, farm machinery, irrigation) to: District/State Cooperative Banks, Regional Rural Banks, Commercial Banks. NABARD also gives direct loans to state governments (RIDF — Rural Infrastructure Development Fund) for rural projects.

questions.question: The Agri-Stack or Digital Agriculture Mission aims to:
options: Create a digital public infrastructure (DPI) for agriculture — farmer registry, crop sown registry, farm boundaries using satellite/GIS to enable precision farming and targeted subsidies
Replace all agricultural workers with robots
Automate MSP procurement
Create digital agricultural exports only
questions.explanation: Digital Agriculture Mission / Agri-Stack (2021-2025): federated farmer database (Farmer ID linked to land records, Aadhaar), Crop Sown Registry (what crops are grown, digitized land records), Remote Sensing and GIS maps of farm plots. Enables: targeted input delivery (seeds, fertilizer), precision advisory (weather, crop insurance, agri-credit).

questions.question: The Asian Infrastructure Investment Bank (AIIB) is:
options: Multilateral development bank proposed by China (2013), established 2015 — 106 members; HQ Beijing; funds infrastructure in Asia; India is the 2nd largest shareholder
ADB branch
World Bank subsidiary
IMF initiative
questions.explanation: AIIB (Asian Infrastructure Investment Bank, 2015): proposed by China's Xi Jinping. 57 founding members. HQ: Beijing. Focus: infrastructure (energy, transport, urban, rural, communications) in Asia. China: largest shareholder (~26%); India: 2nd largest (~8%). The US and Japan did not join (seeing it as China's geopolitical tool). India has received several AIIB loans for transport and urban infrastructure.

questions.question: India's first Regional Rural Bank (RRB) was:
options: Prathama Grameen Bank, established October 2, 1975 in Moradabad, Uttar Pradesh — sponsored by Syndicate Bank
SBI Gramin Bank
Syndicate Bank RRB
Punjab National Bank Rural Bank
questions.explanation: Prathama Bank (now Prathama UP Gramin Bank after merger): first RRB established October 2, 1975, Gandhi Jayanti, in Moradabad, UP. Sponsored by Syndicate Bank. 5 RRBs simultaneously established as pilot. Based on Narasimham Committee recommendations (1975). Total 5 initial RRBs: Prathama (Moradabad), Gorakhpur Kshetriya Gramin (UP), Haryana Kshetriya Gramin (Bhiwani), Jaipur Nagaur Anchalik (Rajasthan), Singhbhum (Bihar).

questions.question: India's 'Silent Revolution' in agriculture refers to:
options: Second Green Revolution for eastern India (Bihar, West Bengal, Odisha, UP) and diversification into horticulture, animal husbandry, and fisheries; also called 'Evergreen Revolution' by M.S. Swaminathan
Original Green Revolution
White Revolution
Blue Revolution
questions.explanation: Second Green Revolution/Evergreen Revolution (M.S. Swaminathan): need to extend agricultural productivity gains to eastern India and dryland farming regions while maintaining ecological sustainability. Also: shift from cereal monoculture to diversification (horticulture, dairying, poultry, fisheries) for nutritional security and farmer income.

questions.question: India's 'Unified Portal for Agricultural Statistics' (UPAg) was launched to:
options: Provide real-time agricultural data — crop sowing area, production estimates, MSP, market prices — to farmers, policymakers, and researchers via a single platform
Replace FCI
Automate MSP procurement
Provide agri loans directly
questions.explanation: UPAg (2022, Ministry of Agriculture): first-of-its-kind single platform integrating agricultural statistics from multiple agencies. Data: crop sowing/area, production, trade, price (wholesale, retail, MSP), input costs, import/export. Makes data accessible to farmers via mobile app. Aims to improve crop planning and reduce information asymmetry.

questions.question: The term 'crop diversification' in agriculture means:
options: Shifting from monoculture to growing multiple crops including horticulture, spices, oilseeds, pulses — reduces risk, improves nutrition, and can increase farmer income compared to wheat/rice only
Growing only wheat and rice
Farming in multiple countries
Diversification of farm ownership
questions.explanation: Crop Diversification: growing a variety of crops (different from traditional cereal monoculture). Benefits: reduces market and weather risk, improves soil health, nutrition security, higher value crops (horticulture → higher income than cereals). Government promotes with MSP for pulses and oilseeds, cluster development programs, and horticulture development programs.

questions.question: The 'double cropping' system in agriculture refers to:
options: Growing two crops per year on the same piece of land — kharif (June-November, monsoon crop: paddy, cotton, maize) + rabi (November-April, winter crop: wheat, mustard, chickpea)
Growing crops in two countries
Two varieties of same crop simultaneously
Mixed cropping of two types
questions.explanation: Double Cropping: two crops sequentially in a year. India's cropping seasons: Kharif (sown with SW monsoon, harvested Oct-Nov): paddy, cotton, maize, soybean, groundnut. Rabi (sown Nov, harvested April): wheat, mustard, chickpea, barley. Zaid (short summer season, March-June): watermelon, cucumber, vegetables. Irrigation enables double/triple cropping (Punjab, Haryana, UP).

questions.question: The Deen Dayal Upadhyaya Grameen Kaushalya Yojana (DDU-GKY) is:
options: Skill training and placement program for rural poor youth (15-35 years, BPL families) — provides free training in 250+ trades, placement guarantee; part of MGNREGA complementary scheme
Scholarship for rural students
Employment guarantee (MGNREGA is that)
Urban skill development
questions.explanation: DDU-GKY (2014, renamed Aajeevika Skills/NRLM Skills): targets rural BPL youth. Free skill training, boarding, lodging during training, and placement assistance. Coverage: 15-35 years (extended to 45 for some groups). Target: place 75%+ trained youth in paid jobs. Implemented by Ministry of Rural Development through State Rural Livelihoods Missions.

questions.question: The National Rural Livelihoods Mission (NRLM/Aajeevika) focuses on:
options: Organizing rural poor into Self-Help Groups (SHGs) — credit linking, livelihood promotion, skill training; targets 10 crore rural poor women in 600 districts; launched 2011
Only individual farmers
Only agricultural credit
Only employment guarantee (MGNREGA does that)
questions.explanation: NRLM (2011, renamed Aajeevika, now renamed DAY-NRLM — Deendayal Antyodaya Yojana): world's largest poverty alleviation program. Promotes SHGs (12-20 women) → federations (village organization) → cluster level federations → block/district level federations. Provides: bank credit linkage (SHG-BLP), livelihood promotion, skill training, financial literacy.

questions.question: The Pradhan Mantri Annadata Aay Sanrakshan Abhiyan (PM-AASHA) scheme comprises:
options: Three sub-schemes: Price Support Scheme (PSS), Price Deficiency Payment Scheme (PDPS), and Private Procurement & Stockist Scheme (PPSS) — to ensure farmers get MSP even in market
Only MSP announcement
Only crop insurance
Only direct income support
questions.explanation: PM-AASHA (2018): umbrella scheme for MSP implementation. PSS: government agencies physically procure crops when prices fall below MSP (for oilseeds, pulses, copra). PDPS: farmers sell in market, government pays difference between MSP and market price directly (for oilseeds). PPSS: private traders can also procure at MSP after paying security deposit (pilot for oilseeds).

questions.question: The 'Lead Bank Scheme' in India was launched to:
options: Assign one bank as 'lead bank' for each district — responsible for coordinating credit plans, financial inclusion, and SHG promotion in that district; launched 1969 on Narashimham Committee recommendation
Provide leadership training to bankers
Make one bank responsible for all banking in a district
Replace cooperative banks
questions.explanation: Lead Bank Scheme (1969): one bank (public sector) designated as lead bank per district (covering all talukas). Functions: preparing annual credit plan (ACP) for the district, coordinating with other banks, promoting financial inclusion, leading SLBC (State Level Bankers' Committee) work in the district. Essential for priority sector credit planning and rural financial inclusion.

questions.question: India's Agriculture Export Policy 2018 targets:
options: $60 billion agricultural exports by 2022 (now extended to 2025) from ~$30 billion baseline — through cluster-based approach, value addition, brand building (GI tags), and export infrastructure
Only rice exports
Only organic farming exports
Import substitution
questions.explanation: Agriculture Export Policy 2018 (Ministry of Commerce): targets doubling agri exports. Key features: Agri Export Zones (AEZ) concept, cluster-based approach (integrated 12 clusters initially), GI (Geographical Indication) products promotion (Darjeeling tea, Basmati rice, Alphonso mango), value addition promotion, infrastructure (cold chain, testing labs), e-commerce for agri exports.

questions.question: The concept of 'mixed economy' in India post-1991 means:
options: Market forces determine most resource allocation; government intervenes in: externalities, public goods, infrastructure, strategic sectors, social sector — less government control than pre-1991 but more than pure laissez-faire
Pure market economy
Pure socialist planned economy
Government controlling all prices
questions.explanation: Post-1991 India: hybrid economy — most industries open to private sector with market-based pricing, but government retains: defence production (DRDO, HAL), atomic energy, nuclear power, railways (mostly), strategic minerals, coal. Also intervenes: agricultural prices (MSP), petroleum prices (partially regulated), utilities (electricity distribution tariff regulation), social sector (healthcare, education). 'Market-friendly' rather than 'market-only.'

questions.question: The Green Revolution (1960s-70s) was associated with:
options: Norman Borlaug (Nobel Peace Prize 1970) — developed semi-dwarf HYV wheat varieties; worked with CIMMYT; brought to India by M.S. Swaminathan and promoted by PM Indira Gandhi's government
Jawaharlal Nehru only
Planning Commission alone
NABARD's initiative
questions.explanation: Green Revolution: Norman Borlaug (USA, Nobel 1970) developed semi-dwarf wheat varieties (higher yield, resistant to lodging — bending with wind). Introduced to India by M.S. Swaminathan (who worked with IARI) under Agriculture Minister C. Subramaniam and PM Indira Gandhi's government. Punjab/Haryana became wheat-surplus states. Pakistan, Mexico also benefited. Also: IR-8 'miracle rice' (IRRI, Philippines).

questions.question: Priority Sector Lending (PSL) requires Indian commercial banks to lend what percentage of ANBC to priority sectors?
questions.explanation: PSL target: 40% of Adjusted Net Bank Credit (ANBC) for domestic scheduled commercial banks. Priority sectors: Agriculture (18%), MSMEs, Education, Housing, Weaker Sections (10%).

questions.question: The Periodic Labour Force Survey (PLFS) in India is conducted by:
options: NITI Aayog
Ministry of Labour
National Statistical Office (NSO)
NSSO under MOSPI
questions.explanation: PLFS is conducted by NSO (National Statistical Office) under MoSPI. Launched 2017-18, provides quarterly urban + annual rural employment/unemployment estimates.

questions.question: The Agriculture Produce Market Committee (APMC) Act was designed to:
options: Export agricultural produce
Protect farmers by regulating markets and preventing exploitation by middlemen
Provide crop insurance
Promote organic farming
questions.explanation: APMC Acts (state subject) created regulated mandis to ensure fair prices for farmers. Critics say they create cartels. The three Farm Acts 2020 (later repealed) aimed to allow sales outside APMCs.

questions.question: e-NAM (electronic National Agriculture Market) is a pan-India online trading platform launched in:
questions.explanation: e-NAM launched April 14, 2016 by SFAC (Small Farmers Agribusiness Consortium) under Ministry of Agriculture. Connects APMC mandis online for transparent price discovery.

questions.question: NABARD's Rural Infrastructure Development Fund (RIDF) provides loans to:
options: Farmers directly for crop loans
State governments for rural infrastructure projects
Commercial banks for priority sector shortfall
Microfinance institutions
questions.explanation: RIDF: state governments and state-owned corporations borrow from NABARD to build rural infrastructure (roads, bridges, irrigation). Funded by shortfall in banks' PSL targets.

questions.question: A price floor is effective (binding) only when it is set:
options: Below the equilibrium price
At the equilibrium price
Above the equilibrium price
At zero
questions.explanation: Price floor (minimum price) is binding only above equilibrium price, creating a surplus (supply > demand). E.g., MSP set above market price creates surplus procurement. Below equilibrium = non-binding.

questions.question: The National Food Security Act (NFSA) 2013 provides subsidized food grains to what percentage of India's population?
questions.explanation: NFSA 2013: covers up to 75% rural + 50% urban population = ~67% of total population (~81.35 crore people). Provides 5 kg/person/month at subsidized rates: Rice Rs 3, Wheat Rs 2, Coarse grains Rs 1/kg.

questions.question: Disguised unemployment is most commonly found in:
options: Agricultural sector — more workers than needed; marginal productivity of labor is zero; removing them does not reduce output
Manufacturing sector
Services sector
Government sector
questions.explanation: Disguised (concealed) unemployment: more people employed than necessary; additional workers add nothing to output (zero marginal product). Common in Indian agriculture (joint farming by family members). Unlike open unemployment (person seeks work but can't find it), disguised unemployment involves apparently 'working' people.

questions.question: India's agricultural marketing is regulated through:
options: State-level APMC (Agricultural Produce Market Committee) Acts — mandated that farmers sell through regulated mandis; APMC reforms (Model APMC Act 2003) and Farm Laws 2020 (repealed 2021) aimed to allow direct selling
Central government alone
RBI
NABARD directly
questions.explanation: APMC (Agricultural Produce Market Committee) system: most states have APMCs that regulate agricultural markets (mandis). Farmers had to sell produce only through licensed commission agents in regulated markets. Critiqued for middleman exploitation. Model APMC Act 2003: allow private markets, direct farmer-buyer links. Farm Laws 2020 (allowing direct selling, contract farming) were repealed November 2021 after farmer protests.

questions.question: The NITI Aayog's 'Aspirational Districts Programme' targets:
options: 112 most underdeveloped districts (selected based on composite index of health, nutrition, education, agriculture, financial inclusion, infrastructure, skill development) to rapidly improve outcomes
All 740 districts
Only tribal districts
Only Northeast districts
questions.explanation: Aspirational Districts Programme (2018): 112 districts ranked on 49 indicators across 5 themes: Health & Nutrition, Education, Agriculture & Water Resources, Financial Inclusion & Skill Development, Basic Infrastructure. Real-time monitoring on Championing Change portal. 'Competitive and cooperative federalism' — districts compete and learn from each other. Success: significant improvement in health, nutrition, education indicators.

questions.question: The ASHA (Accredited Social Health Activist) programme was launched under which mission?
options: ICDS
National Health Mission (NHM)
Ayushman Bharat
National Rural Livelihood Mission
questions.explanation: ASHA workers: introduced under National Rural Health Mission (NRHM, 2005) — now NHM. ~10 lakh ASHAs serve as community health workers, linking villages to the healthcare system.

questions.question: Minimum Support Price (MSP) for agricultural crops in India is recommended by which of the following bodies?
options: Reserve Bank of India
NITI Aayog
Commission for Agricultural Costs and Prices (CACP)
National Bank for Agriculture and Rural Development (NABARD)
questions.explanation: CACP recommends MSP for 22 mandatory crops and sugarcane (where FRP applies), based on factors like cost of production, demand-supply, and international prices.

questions.question: The primary objective of establishing Agricultural Produce Market Committee (APMC) mandis was to:
options: Promote contract farming exclusively
Eliminate all middlemen from agricultural trade
Regulate markets and protect farmers from exploitation by intermediaries
Facilitate direct export of farm produce
questions.explanation: APMC mandis were set up to ensure fair prices, prevent exploitation by middlemen, and regulate agricultural trade through licensed buyers and regulated market yards.

questions.question: The Targeted Public Distribution System (TPDS) was introduced in India with the objective of:
options: Providing free food grains to all citizens
Restricting food subsidy to identified poor households
Privatizing the public distribution system
Replacing food grains with cash transfers to all farmers
questions.explanation: TPDS, introduced in 1997, focused subsidies on identified Below Poverty Line (BPL) families, distinguishing them from Above Poverty Line (APL) households to reduce fiscal burden.

questions.question: The buffer stock operations of the Food Corporation of India (FCI) are primarily aimed at:
options: Exporting food grains to earn foreign exchange
Supporting industrial use of food grains
Meeting contingencies and ensuring national food security
Providing raw materials to agro-based industries
questions.explanation: FCI maintains buffer stocks to meet exigencies like natural calamities, production shortfalls, and ensure food security through the Public Distribution System.

questions.question: Which of the following are the three pillars of the WTO's Agreement on Agriculture (AoA)?
options: Domestic support, market access, and export competition
Import tariffs, export subsidies, and food safety
Subsidies, taxation, and procurement
Plant protection, animal health, and food safety
questions.explanation: The AoA covers three main areas: domestic support (subsidies), market access (tariffs and quotas), and export competition (export subsidies and credits).

questions.question: The Pradhan Mantri Kisan Samman Nidhi (PM-KISAN) scheme provides direct income support to eligible farmer families. The annual assistance provided is:
options: Rs. 4,000 per year
Rs. 6,000 per year
Rs. 8,000 per year
Rs. 12,000 per year
questions.explanation: PM-KISAN provides Rs. 6,000 annually in three equal installments of Rs. 2,000, transferred directly to bank accounts of eligible landholding farmer families.

questions.question: The Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana (PMFBY) is considered an improvement over previous crop insurance schemes primarily because it:
options: Covers only commercial cash crops
Is mandatory for all farmers irrespective of credit status
Uses technology for yield estimation and caps premium paid by farmers
Is implemented exclusively by private insurance companies
questions.explanation: PMFBY uses remote sensing, drones, and smartphones for accurate crop cutting experiments, and caps farmer premium at 2% (Kharif) and 1.5% (Rabi) of sum insured.

questions.question: The electronic National Agriculture Market (e-NAM) is an initiative that primarily aims to:
options: Provide direct income support to farmers
Create a unified online platform for inter-mandi and inter-state agricultural trade
Replace all physical mandis with private yards
Distribute free seeds and fertilizers online
questions.explanation: e-NAM is a pan-India electronic trading portal linking existing APMC mandis to create a unified national market, enabling transparent online price discovery and competition.

questions.question: The Paramparagat Krishi Vikas Yojana (PKVY) is an agricultural development scheme that promotes:
options: Chemical-intensive farming to boost productivity
Organic farming through cluster-based approach
Genetically modified crop cultivation
Corporate contract farming models
questions.explanation: PKVY promotes organic farming through cluster approach, encouraging groups of farmers to adopt traditional and chemical-free cultivation methods with financial assistance.

questions.question: Institutional credit to agriculture in India is primarily provided by:
options: Money lenders and commission agents
Commercial Banks, RRBs, and Cooperative Credit Societies
Foreign institutional investors
Microfinance institutions only
questions.explanation: Institutional credit comes from Commercial Banks, Regional Rural Banks (RRBs), and Cooperative Credit Societies. The government aims to reduce dependence on non-institutional sources like money lenders.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the impact of a 'Minimum Support Price' (MSP) on the market equilibrium of a specific crop in India?
options: It shifts the demand curve to the right, increasing consumption.
It creates a price floor that can lead to surplus production if set above the equilibrium price.
It acts as a price ceiling to protect consumers from inflation.
It eliminates the need for government procurement operations.
questions.explanation: MSP acts as a price floor; when set above market equilibrium, it encourages overproduction, leading to surpluses.

questions.question: In the context of Indian agriculture, what is the primary objective of 'Zero Budget Natural Farming' (ZBNF)?
options: To maximize yield through high-intensity chemical fertilization.
To reduce the cost of production by eliminating reliance on synthetic inputs.
To shift the entire agricultural focus toward export-oriented cash crops.
To introduce genetically modified seeds to replace traditional varieties.
questions.explanation: ZBNF aims to reduce input costs and environmental impact by avoiding chemical fertilizers and pesticides.

questions.question: How does an increase in the 'Terms of Trade' for agricultural products typically affect the rural economy?
options: It reduces the purchasing power of farmers relative to industrial goods.
It increases the real income of farmers as the price of agricultural outputs rises relative to inputs.
It leads to a decrease in the overall GDP contribution of the primary sector.
It causes a shift from commercial farming to subsistence farming.
questions.explanation: Improved terms of trade mean agricultural prices rise faster than industrial prices, increasing farmers' real income.

questions.question: Which of the following is the most likely consequence of 'over-irrigation' in the Punjab-Haryana region?
options: Increase in the soil's capacity to retain organic carbon.
Secondary salinization and degradation of soil health.
Reduction in the groundwater extraction rate.
Elimination of the need for chemical fertilizers.
questions.explanation: Excessive irrigation leads to waterlogging and capillary action that brings salts to the surface, causing salinization.

questions.question: What is the conceptual significance of the 'Agricultural Terms of Trade' (ToT) in analyzing rural distress?
options: It measures the ratio of agricultural exports to total national exports.
It reflects the ratio of prices received by farmers to prices paid by them for non-agricultural goods.
It tracks the movement of labor from agriculture to the manufacturing sector.
It measures the total credit flow from NABARD to small farmers.
questions.explanation: ToT measures the relative price of agricultural outputs versus industrial inputs, indicating the farmers' purchasing power.

questions.question: Which of the following statements regarding 'Contract Farming' is correct in the context of agricultural reforms?
options: It completely removes the risk of crop failure from the farmer's burden.
It aims to integrate farmers directly with the processing industry to reduce middleman margins.
It mandates that the government must buy all produce at the MSP.
It prohibits the use of private investment in agricultural infrastructure.
questions.explanation: Contract farming links producers directly to buyers, reducing transaction costs and market uncertainty.

questions.question: What is the primary reason why 'Crop Insurance' (like PMFBY) is considered a critical tool for agricultural risk management?
options: It guarantees a fixed profit regardless of the harvest quality.
It protects farmers against systemic risks like droughts and floods to prevent debt traps.
It eliminates the need for farmers to seek loans from formal banking channels.
It mandates the use of high-yielding variety seeds.
questions.explanation: Insurance mitigates the financial impact of unpredictable climatic shocks, preventing farmers from falling into chronic debt.

questions.question: What is the effect of 'Agricultural Diversification' on the economic resilience of a rural household?
options: It increases dependence on a single crop, reducing market risk.
It spreads risk across multiple income streams, reducing vulnerability to price volatility.
It leads to a decrease in the total productivity of the land.
It forces farmers to rely solely on government subsidies for survival.
questions.explanation: Diversification reduces risk by ensuring that a failure in one crop or market does not wipe out all income.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the 'Cobweb Phenomenon' in agricultural economics?
options: The gradual transition from traditional farming to organic farming.
Price fluctuations caused by a lag between the production decision and the actual harvest.
The process of land consolidation to increase the size of holdings.
The impact of global trade barriers on the export of cereals.
questions.explanation: The Cobweb model explains price volatility where producers base current production on previous prices, causing cycles of surplus and shortage.

questions.question: In the context of Indian agriculture, what does the 'hidden unemployment' or 'disguised unemployment' imply?
options: Farmers are unemployed because there is a lack of demand for crops.
More people are engaged in farming than are actually required, meaning marginal productivity is zero.
Laborers are unable to find work due to the mechanization of agriculture.
Farmers are working in sectors other than agriculture but are counted as agriculturalists.
questions.explanation: Disguised unemployment occurs when additional labor does not increase total output, indicating underemployment.

questions.question: What is the likely impact of rupee depreciation on agricultural exports?
options: A) Increase due to higher competitiveness
B) Decrease due to higher input costs
C) No change as exports are inelastic
D) Volatile due to exchange rate fluctuations
questions.explanation: Rupee depreciation makes agricultural exports cheaper abroad, boosting demand.

questions.question: How do sterilized interventions affect agricultural lending?
options: A) Reduce credit availability by tightening liquidity
B) Increase crop insurance uptake
C) Lower interest rates for farm loans
D) Stabilize MSP prices
questions.explanation: Sterilized interventions reduce money supply, raising interest rates and limiting farm loans.

questions.question: What is a primary goal of Minimum Support Price (MSP) reforms?
options: A) Ensure farmer income stability
B) Encourage export-oriented production
C) Reduce government subsidy burden
D) Promote organic farming
questions.explanation: MSP aims to protect farmers from price volatility by setting a floor price.

questions.question: Which fiscal indicator directly reflects agricultural subsidies?
options: A) Current account deficit
B) Food grain reserves
C) Subsidy outlay as % of GDP
D) Balance of payments surplus
questions.explanation: Subsidy outlay as % of GDP quantifies fiscal support to agriculture.

questions.question: What factor could trigger agricultural inflation?
options: A) Surplus production from monsoon
B) Export ban on pulses
C) Increased use of fertilizers
D) Lower import tariffs on grains
questions.explanation: Export bans reduce domestic supply, driving up prices.

questions.question: How does increased agricultural exports affect trade balance?
options: A) Worsen deficit by raising import dependency
B) Improve surplus through higher export earnings
C) Neutralize impact via currency adjustments
D) Create volatility in commodity prices
questions.explanation: Higher exports increase foreign exchange earnings, improving trade balance.

questions.question: What social indicator reflects farmer distress in rural India?
options: A) Rural employment ratio
B) Agricultural credit growth
C) Food security index
D) Urbanization rate
questions.explanation: Food security index measures access to affordable nutrition, indicating distress.

questions.question: Which monetary policy tool influences farm input costs?
options: A) Reserve requirements for banks
B) Open market operations on government bonds
C) Direct subsidies to cooperatives
D) Import duty on machinery
questions.explanation: Open market operations affect interest rates, impacting loan costs for seeds/fertilizers.

questions.question: What is a consequence of reducing public investment in irrigation?
options: A) Higher crop yields due to farmer adaptation
B) Increased vulnerability to drought
C) Lower food prices from private sector efficiency
D) Improved groundwater recharge
questions.explanation: Reduced irrigation investment raises drought risks, threatening farm output.

questions.question: How do land reforms improve agricultural productivity?
options: A) By consolidating small holdings into large farms
B) By redistributing land to marginal farmers
C) By abolishing cooperative farming
D) By introducing genetically modified crops
questions.explanation: Land redistribution to marginal farmers enhances access to resources and productivity.

questions.question: What role does technology adoption play in agriculture?
options: A) Reduces labor demand but increases output
B) Increases input costs without yield gains
C) Is irrelevant to smallholder farmers
D) Guarantees food security
questions.explanation: Technology adoption boosts output efficiency, even with reduced labor.

questions.question: In the context of the Indian economy, which of the following best describes the 'Terms of Trade' for the agricultural sector?
options: The ratio of agricultural exports to agricultural imports
The ratio of the index of prices received by farmers to the index of prices paid by farmers
The difference between the Minimum Support Price and the market price
The proportion of agricultural GDP to the total national GDP
questions.explanation: Terms of Trade measure the purchasing power of farmers by comparing prices of outputs received versus inputs paid.

questions.question: Which of the following is the most likely impact of a significant depreciation of the Indian Rupee on the agricultural trade balance?
options: Decrease in the competitiveness of agricultural exports in the global market
Increase in the cost of imported fertilizers and pesticides
Automatic decrease in the domestic price of food grains
Reduction in the total value of agricultural exports in foreign currency
questions.explanation: Rupee depreciation makes imports more expensive, increasing the cost of imported inputs like potash and phosphates.

questions.question: The concept of 'Minimum Support Price' (MSP) in India is primarily designed to achieve which of the following economic objectives?
options: To eliminate the role of middlemen in the supply chain
To ensure a floor price to protect farmers against price crashes during bumper harvests
To regulate the consumption patterns of the urban population
To ensure that agricultural products are exported at competitive global prices
questions.explanation: MSP acts as a safety net to prevent distress sales by guaranteeing a minimum price for specific crops.

questions.question: Which of the following scenarios would most likely lead to 'Cost-Push Inflation' in the agricultural sector?
options: A sudden increase in the demand for organic produce
An increase in the government's procurement quota
A sharp rise in the prices of diesel and chemical fertilizers
A decrease in the interest rates on agricultural loans
questions.explanation: Cost-push inflation occurs when rising production costs (inputs) force producers to increase the final price of goods.

questions.question: With reference to 'Agricultural Credit' in India, what is the primary objective of the Priority Sector Lending (PSL) mandates?
options: To ensure that only large-scale farmers receive credit
To direct credit flow to sectors that are neglected by commercial banks
To eliminate the need for collateral in all agricultural loans
To shift the entire burden of agricultural lending to the RBI
questions.explanation: PSL ensures that credit reaches essential sectors like agriculture and MSMEs that might otherwise be ignored by banks.

questions.question: How does a 'Buffer Stock' maintained by the Food Corporation of India (FCI) help in stabilizing the economy?
options: By ensuring that prices remain constant regardless of demand
By mitigating price volatility through strategic release during shortages
By eliminating the need for the Public Distribution System (PDS)
By preventing the government from intervening in the open market
questions.explanation: Buffer stocks allow the government to release grain during scarcity to prevent price spikes and ensure food security.

questions.question: What is the economic implication of 'Crop Diversification' from water-intensive crops to oilseeds or pulses?
options: Increase in the depletion of the groundwater table
Reduction in the overall agricultural GDP
Improvement in soil health and reduction in ecological stress
Increase in the dependence on chemical fertilizers
questions.explanation: Diversification reduces the over-reliance on water-intensive crops like paddy, improving environmental sustainability.

questions.question: Which of the following best explains the 'Cobweb Phenomenon' in agricultural markets?
options: The process of diversifying crops to increase yield
The lag between the decision to produce and the actual harvest leading to price fluctuations
The impact of subsidies on the production of cash crops
The movement of agricultural goods from rural to urban markets
questions.explanation: The Cobweb model explains price volatility caused by producers basing current production on previous period prices.

questions.question: In the context of agricultural reforms, what is the primary goal of promoting 'Contract Farming'?
options: To make the government the sole buyer of all agricultural produce
To reduce the risk for the farmer by fixing prices before the harvest
To eliminate the use of technology in farming
To stop the export of agricultural goods to other countries
questions.explanation: Contract farming shifts some production risk from the farmer to the buyer through pre-agreed prices.

questions.question: Which of the following is a characteristic of 'Disguised Unemployment' often found in Indian agriculture?
options: People are unemployed due to a lack of available jobs
Workers are employed but their marginal productivity is zero
Workers are unemployed for a few months of the year
Farmers are unable to find markets for their produce
questions.explanation: Disguised unemployment occurs when more people are engaged in an activity than are actually required.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the impact of the 'Minimum Support Price' (MSP) on the cropping pattern of Indian farmers?
options: It encourages diversification towards oilseeds and pulses
It leads to an over-reliance on water-intensive crops like paddy and wheat
It eliminates the risk of price volatility for all horticultural crops
It reduces the government's fiscal burden on food procurement
questions.explanation: MSP for wheat and paddy incentivizes farmers to grow these crops over others, often leading to groundwater depletion in semi-arid regions.

questions.question: In the context of agricultural financing, what is the primary objective of the 'Priority Sector Lending' (PSL) targets for banks?
options: To maximize the profit margins of commercial banks
To ensure credit flow to sectors that are often neglected by formal banking
To replace the need for institutional credit with microfinance
To limit the exposure of banks to the agricultural sector
questions.explanation: PSL ensures that a specific percentage of bank lending goes to sectors like agriculture to promote inclusive growth.

questions.question: Which of the following statements regarding 'Agricultural Terms of Trade' (ToT) is correct?
options: A rise in ToT implies that the prices of industrial goods are rising faster than agricultural prices
A fall in ToT indicates that farmers can buy more industrial goods for the same amount of agricultural produce
A rise in ToT means the ratio of prices received by farmers to prices paid by them has increased
ToT is independent of the inflation rate in the manufacturing sector
questions.explanation: Terms of Trade improve when the price of agricultural output rises relative to the price of non-agricultural inputs.

questions.question: How does the 'Open Market Operations' (OMO) conducted by the RBI indirectly affect agricultural investment?
options: By directly funding the PM-KISAN scheme
By influencing liquidity and the cost of borrowing for agri-loans
By fixing the MSP for Kharif crops
By regulating the export quotas of agricultural commodities
questions.explanation: OMO affects systemic liquidity, which influences interest rates, thereby impacting the cost of loans for agricultural investments.

questions.question: What is the primary economic rationale behind the 'Payment on Account' system in the procurement of food grains?
options: To increase the storage capacity of warehouses
To provide immediate liquidity to farmers before the final quality assay
To discourage the production of surplus grains
To eliminate the role of middlemen in the Mandi system
questions.explanation: Payment on account provides farmers with immediate cash flow during the harvest season before the final weight and quality are verified.

questions.question: With reference to 'Contract Farming', which of the following is a potential economic risk for the small-scale farmer?
options: Elimination of market price volatility
Reduced dependence on traditional seeds
Asymmetry of bargaining power and risk of contract breach
Guaranteed purchase of the entire produce
questions.explanation: Small farmers often lack the legal resources to negotiate fair terms or enforce contracts against large corporate buyers.

questions.question: If the Government of India increases the export duty on onions, what is the most likely immediate effect on the domestic economy?
options: Domestic prices of onions are likely to increase
Domestic supply of onions will decrease
Domestic prices of onions are likely to decrease
Foreign demand for Indian onions will increase
questions.explanation: Higher export duties discourage exports, increasing domestic supply and thereby lowering the domestic price.

questions.question: Which of the following is a characteristic of 'Zero Budget Natural Farming' (ZBNF) from an economic perspective?
options: Increased dependence on chemical fertilizers to maximize yield
Reduction in the cost of cultivation by eliminating external inputs
Increase in the fiscal subsidy burden on the government
Shift from food crops to commercial cash crops
questions.explanation: ZBNF aims to reduce the cost of production by replacing chemical inputs with on-farm biological preparations.

questions.question: What is the impact of a depreciation of the Indian Rupee on the competitiveness of Indian agricultural exports?
options: It makes Indian agri-products more expensive in the international market
It makes Indian agri-products cheaper and more competitive globally
It has no effect as agricultural products are priced in local currency
It leads to an immediate decrease in the volume of exports
questions.explanation: A weaker rupee makes exports cheaper for foreign buyers, potentially increasing the volume of agricultural exports.

questions.question: The 'Buffer Stock' maintained by the Food Corporation of India (FCI) primarily serves which of the following economic functions?
options: To ensure the monopoly of the government in grain trade
To stabilize food prices and ensure food security during shortages
To maximize the profit from the sale of grains in the global market
To reduce the need for cold storage infrastructure
questions.explanation: Buffer stocks are used to manage supply shocks and prevent extreme price volatility in the domestic market.

questions.question: With reference to the Minimum Support Price (MSP) in India, which of the following statements best describes its primary economic objective?
options: To ensure the consumer gets essential food grains at subsidized rates
To protect farmers against sharp falls in agricultural prices during bumper harvests
To eliminate the role of middlemen in the agricultural marketing chain
To incentivize the shift from food grains to commercial cash crops
questions.explanation: MSP acts as a price floor to prevent farmers' income from crashing during periods of overproduction.

questions.question: In the context of Indian agriculture, what is the primary economic implication of 'Crop Diversification'?
options: Increasing the total area under monoculture to achieve economies of scale
Reducing the dependency on groundwater by shifting to rain-fed crops
Mitigating systemic risk and improving soil health by rotating different crop types
Increasing the reliance on chemical fertilizers to boost yield per hectare
questions.explanation: Diversification reduces the risk of total crop failure and prevents soil nutrient depletion caused by monocropping.

questions.question: Which of the following would be the most likely impact of a significant depreciation of the Indian Rupee on the agricultural sector?
options: Decrease in the competitiveness of agricultural exports in the global market
Increase in the cost of imported fertilizers and pesticides
Reduction in the domestic prices of exported agricultural commodities
Decrease in the total revenue earned by exporters of basmati rice
questions.explanation: Rupee depreciation makes imports more expensive, increasing the cost of imported inputs like potash and phosphate.

questions.question: Regarding the 'Agriculture Infrastructure Fund' (AIF), which of the following is its core conceptual objective?
options: Providing direct income support to small farmers through cash transfers
Financing the creation of post-harvest management infrastructure to reduce wastage
Subsidizing the purchase of high-yield variety (HYV) seeds
Regulating the retail prices of essential commodities in urban markets
questions.explanation: AIF focuses on creating assets like cold storages and warehouses to reduce post-harvest losses.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the 'Terms of Trade' (ToT) for agriculture in the context of the Indian economy?
options: The ratio of the price of agricultural exports to the price of agricultural imports
The ratio of the price received by farmers for their produce relative to the price of non-agricultural goods
The difference between the MSP and the market price of a specific crop
The ratio of total agricultural production to total industrial production
questions.explanation: ToT measures the purchasing power of the agricultural sector relative to the industrial sector.

questions.question: Consider the impact of 'Price Ceiling' imposed by the government on an essential agricultural commodity. What is the most likely economic outcome?
options: An increase in the supply of the commodity due to higher demand
The emergence of black markets and potential shortages
A decrease in the demand for the commodity among low-income groups
An increase in the incentive for farmers to produce more of that commodity
questions.explanation: Price ceilings below equilibrium levels lead to excess demand and shortage, often fueling black markets.

questions.question: Which of the following is a characteristic of 'Contract Farming' as an economic model?
options: The farmer bears all the market price risk while the company provides only seeds
A pre-agreed price is fixed between the farmer and the buyer before the sowing season
The government acts as the sole buyer and price-setter for the produce
It eliminates the need for any formal legal agreement between the parties
questions.explanation: Contract farming transfers price risk from the farmer to the buyer via pre-fixed pricing.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of 'Sterilized Intervention' by the RBI in the context of agricultural export surges?
options: To prevent the appreciation of the rupee that could hurt export competitiveness
To increase the inflation rate to benefit the farmers' income
To reduce the amount of foreign exchange reserves held by the central bank
To discourage farmers from producing export-oriented crops
questions.explanation: RBI buys foreign currency to stop rupee appreciation and sells bonds to mop up excess liquidity.

questions.question: How does the 'PM-KISAN' scheme differ conceptually from the 'PM Fasal Bima Yojana'?
options: PM-KISAN is a credit-linked subsidy, while PMFBY is a direct cash transfer
PM-KISAN provides income support, while PMFBY provides risk coverage against crop failure
PM-KISAN is a crop-specific insurance, while PMFBY is a general income support
PM-KISAN is managed by the RBI, while PMFBY is managed by NABARD
questions.explanation: PM-KISAN is a direct benefit transfer for income support, whereas PMFBY is an insurance mechanism.

questions.question: In the context of agricultural economics, what does the 'Law of Diminishing Marginal Returns' imply when applying more fertilizer to a fixed plot of land?
options: Total production will decrease immediately after the first dose of fertilizer
The additional increase in yield will eventually decline as more fertilizer is added
The cost of fertilizer will decrease as the quantity used increases
The total yield will increase linearly regardless of the amount of fertilizer used
questions.explanation: Adding more of one input while others remain constant leads to a decrease in the marginal output.

questions.question: With reference to the Minimum Support Price (MSP), which of the following statements best describes its primary economic objective in the Indian agricultural context?
options: To eliminate the role of middlemen in the agricultural supply chain
To provide a price floor to protect farmers against sharp falls in agricultural prices
To ensure that the consumer price of essential food grains remains constant
To incentivize farmers to shift from food crops to commercial cash crops
questions.explanation: MSP acts as a price floor to ensure farmers receive a minimum guaranteed price, preventing distress sales during bumper harvests.

questions.question: In the context of Indian agriculture, what is the most likely impact of a significant depreciation of the Indian Rupee on the agri-economy?
options: Decrease in the competitiveness of agricultural exports in the global market
Increase in the domestic cost of imported fertilizers and pesticides
Reduction in the overall profitability of organic farming exports
Decrease in the incentive for farmers to produce export-oriented crops
questions.explanation: Rupee depreciation makes imports more expensive, increasing the cost of imported inputs like potash and phosphate fertilizers.

questions.question: Which of the following scenarios would most likely lead to 'Cost-Push Inflation' specifically within the agricultural sector?
options: A sudden increase in the demand for organic pulses due to health trends
An increase in the government's procurement quota for wheat
A sharp rise in the prices of diesel and electricity used for irrigation
An increase in the Minimum Support Price for paddy
questions.explanation: Cost-push inflation occurs when the cost of production inputs (like fuel and power) rises, forcing prices upward regardless of demand.

questions.question: Which of the following is a characteristic of 'Contract Farming' as a reform measure in Indian agriculture?
options: Complete elimination of the farmer's ownership over the land
Transfer of price risk from the farmer to the buyer through pre-agreed prices
Requirement for the farmer to sell only to government procurement agencies
A system where the government guarantees the profit margin for the farmer
questions.explanation: Contract farming reduces price volatility for farmers by fixing the purchase price before the sowing season begins.

questions.question: The 'Terms of Trade' for agriculture are said to be deteriorating when:
options: The price of agricultural goods rises faster than the price of industrial goods
The volume of agricultural exports exceeds the volume of agricultural imports
The price of industrial goods rises faster than the price of agricultural goods
The government increases the subsidy on fertilizers while decreasing MSP
questions.explanation: Deteriorating terms of trade occur when the relative price of agricultural outputs falls compared to the industrial inputs farmers must buy.

questions.question: Which of the following best explains the 'Cobweb Phenomenon' in the context of agricultural price cycles?
options: The tendency of farmers to diversify crops to avoid monoculture
The lag between the price signal and the production response leading to price fluctuations
The impact of seasonal rainfall on the yield of Kharif crops
The correlation between global commodity prices and domestic procurement prices
questions.explanation: The Cobweb model describes how farmers base current production on past prices, leading to alternating cycles of surplus and shortage.

questions.question: How does an increase in the 'Cash Reserve Ratio' (CRR) by the RBI potentially affect the agricultural credit market?
options: It increases the liquidity available with banks to provide short-term crop loans
It reduces the cost of borrowing for farmers through lower interest rates
It may lead to a tightening of credit availability for agricultural loans
It directly increases the amount of subsidies provided to farmers
questions.explanation: Higher CRR reduces the loanable funds available with commercial banks, potentially limiting credit flow to the agri-sector.

questions.question: What is the primary economic rationale behind providing 'Power Subsidies' for irrigation in India?
options: To encourage the use of water-intensive crops like sugarcane and paddy
To reduce the fiscal deficit of the state governments
To lower the cost of production and ensure food security for small farmers
To eliminate the need for rainwater harvesting and groundwater management
questions.explanation: Power subsidies aim to reduce production costs for farmers, making farming viable and ensuring stable food supply.

questions.question: Which of the following is a potential consequence of 'Over-production' of a specific crop due to high MSP?
options: A shift toward crop diversification and sustainable farming
Ecological degradation due to excessive groundwater extraction
A decrease in the government's food grain procurement bill
An immediate increase in the global market price of that specific crop
questions.explanation: High MSP for specific crops (like paddy) leads to over-cultivation, causing depletion of groundwater and soil health.

questions.question: In the context of the Balance of Payments, an increase in the export of high-value agricultural products like Basmati rice would:
options: Increase the deficit in the Capital Account
Lead to a credit in the Current Account of the Balance of Payments
Decrease the foreign exchange reserves of the country
Increase the demand for imports of industrial machinery
questions.explanation: Agricultural exports are recorded as credits in the merchandise trade section of the Current Account.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the 'Minimum Support Price' (MSP) mechanism in India as a tool of agricultural economics?
options: A price ceiling to protect consumers from inflation
A price floor to ensure a guaranteed return to farmers
A variable subsidy linked to the global market price
A quota system to limit the production of specific crops
questions.explanation: MSP acts as a price floor, preventing prices from falling below a certain level to protect farmers' income.

questions.question: In the context of Indian agriculture, what is the primary economic objective of 'Crop Diversification'?
options: To increase the reliance on a single high-yielding variety
To reduce the risk of total crop failure and improve soil health
To eliminate the need for irrigation systems
To shift entirely from food crops to commercial cash crops
questions.explanation: Diversification mitigates systemic risk from pests or weather and restores soil nutrients depleted by monoculture.

questions.question: How does an increase in the 'Terms of Trade' for agriculture generally impact the rural economy?
options: It reduces the purchasing power of farmers relative to industrial goods
It increases the real income of farmers as agricultural prices rise relative to non-agricultural prices
It leads to a decrease in the overall demand for agricultural produce
It forces farmers to shift from commercial crops to subsistence farming
questions.explanation: Improved terms of trade mean agricultural prices rise faster than industrial prices, increasing farmers' purchasing power.

questions.question: Which of the following is a likely outcome of 'Over-production' of a specific crop under a guaranteed MSP regime?
options: Automatic decrease in the production of that crop
Increased efficiency in the allocation of land resources
Market distortions leading to inefficient resource allocation
Immediate reduction in the government's fiscal deficit
questions.explanation: Guaranteed prices encourage over-production of specific crops, leading to resource wastage and environmental degradation.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of 'Agricultural Credit' through the Kisan Credit Card (KCC) scheme?
options: To provide long-term loans for land purchase
To provide timely credit for short-term production needs
To replace all forms of institutional credit with government grants
To fund the complete industrialization of rural areas
questions.explanation: KCC is designed to meet short-term credit requirements for cultivation and post-harvest expenses.

questions.question: Which of the following best explains the concept of 'Agricultural Terms of Trade' (ToT)?
options: The ratio of agricultural exports to agricultural imports
The ratio of prices received by farmers to the prices paid by them for other goods
The total value of agricultural GDP relative to the national GDP
The difference between the MSP and the market price of crops
questions.explanation: ToT measures the relative price of agricultural outputs compared to the prices of industrial/service inputs.

questions.question: What is the economic implication of 'Fragmented Landholdings' in Indian agriculture?
options: Increased ease of implementing large-scale mechanization
Lower transaction costs for farmers seeking credit
Diseconomies of scale and reduced productivity per unit of land
Automatic increase in the efficiency of irrigation distribution
questions.explanation: Small, fragmented plots prevent the use of modern machinery and increase the cost of cultivation per hectare.

questions.question: Which of the following is a direct effect of 'Input Subsidies' (like fertilizer or power subsidies) on agricultural production?
options: It increases the cost of production for the farmer
It encourages the optimal and sustainable use of resources
It lowers the cost of production, potentially leading to over-utilization of inputs
It eliminates the need for government procurement agencies
questions.explanation: Subsidies lower the marginal cost of inputs, often leading to inefficient over-use (e.g., excessive urea use).

questions.question: In the context of agricultural marketing, what is the main objective of 'e-NAM' (Electronic National Agriculture Market)?
options: To fix a single price for all crops across India
To create a unified national market to reduce intermediaries and ensure better price discovery
To ban the private trade of agricultural commodities
To provide free insurance to all registered farmers
questions.explanation: e-NAM aims to remove trade barriers and intermediaries by connecting mandis digitally for transparent pricing.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the 'Agricultural Productivity Gap'?
options: The difference between the MSP and the market price
The difference between potential yield and the actual yield achieved by farmers
The gap between agricultural GDP and industrial GDP
The difference between the amount of credit sanctioned and the amount disbursed
questions.explanation: The productivity gap is the difference between what a crop could produce under ideal conditions and what is actually produced.

questions.question: With reference to the Minimum Support Price (MSP), which of the following statements best describes its primary objective in the Indian economy?
options: To eliminate the role of middlemen in the agricultural supply chain
To provide a price floor to protect farmers against sharp falls in agricultural prices
To regulate the total quantity of food grains produced annually
To ensure that agricultural produce is exported at competitive global rates
questions.explanation: MSP acts as a price floor to ensure farmers receive a minimum guaranteed price, preventing distress sales during bumper harvests.

questions.question: How does an increase in the 'Terms of Trade' for agriculture typically impact the rural economy of India?
options: It decreases the purchasing power of farmers relative to industrial goods
It leads to a decrease in the real income of the agricultural sector
It increases the purchasing power of farmers as agricultural prices rise relative to non-agricultural prices
It causes an immediate shift from food crops to cash crops regardless of soil quality
questions.explanation: Improved Terms of Trade mean agricultural prices rise faster than industrial prices, increasing the real income of farmers.

questions.question: Which of the following is a characteristic of 'Zero Budget Natural Farming' (ZBNF) as promoted in India?
options: Heavy reliance on chemical fertilizers to maximize yield per hectare
Complete elimination of all forms of organic manure
Avoidance of synthetic chemical inputs to reduce the cost of cultivation
Focus on mono-cropping to achieve economies of scale
questions.explanation: ZBNF focuses on reducing input costs by eliminating synthetic chemicals and utilizing on-farm biological resources.

questions.question: In the context of Indian agriculture, what is the primary economic rationale behind the 'Crop Diversification' strategy?
options: To increase the dependence on a single high-yielding variety of wheat
To mitigate the risk of crop failure and reduce the ecological pressure of mono-cropping
To ensure that only export-oriented crops are grown in the plains
To eliminate the need for irrigation systems in rain-fed areas
questions.explanation: Diversification reduces systemic risk and prevents soil nutrient depletion caused by continuous cultivation of the same crop.

questions.question: Consider the impact of a 'Buffer Stock' maintained by the Food Corporation of India (FCI). Which of the following is a primary economic function of this mechanism?
options: To maximize the profit margins of the government in the international market
To stabilize food prices and ensure food security during lean periods
To discourage the production of pulses and oilseeds
To shift the entire burden of storage costs onto the small-scale farmers
questions.explanation: Buffer stocks are used to manage supply shocks and stabilize prices through open market operations.

questions.question: What is the primary objective of the 'PM-KISAN' scheme in the context of agricultural finance?
options: To provide low-interest loans for purchasing heavy machinery
To provide direct income support to supplement the financial needs of landholding farmers
To provide insurance coverage against crop failure due to natural calamities
To subsidize the cost of chemical fertilizers and pesticides
questions.explanation: PM-KISAN is a Central Sector scheme providing direct cash transfers to farmers for income support.

questions.question: Which of the following factors most directly contributes to the 'disguised unemployment' often observed in the Indian agricultural sector?
options: A shortage of labor during the harvesting season
The use of advanced automation reducing the need for human labor
The absorption of more labor than is required for the available land
The migration of rural youth to urban industrial centers
questions.explanation: Disguised unemployment occurs when more people are employed than necessary, resulting in zero marginal productivity of labor.

questions.question: In the context of agricultural marketing, what is the primary goal of the 'e-NAM' (Electronic National Agriculture Market) platform?
options: To fix a single price for all crops across the country regardless of quality
To create a unified national market to remove intermediaries and ensure better price discovery
To restrict the sale of agricultural produce to government agencies only
To eliminate the use of traditional mandis entirely within one year
questions.explanation: e-NAM aims to integrate markets nationwide to ensure transparency and better price realization for farmers.

questions.question: How does the 'Priority Sector Lending' (PSL) norms of the RBI impact the agricultural sector?
options: It forces banks to lend only to large-scale corporate farms
It mandates banks to allocate a specific percentage of their lending to agriculture and allied activities
It prohibits banks from providing loans to the agricultural sector
It ensures that all agricultural loans are provided at a 0% interest rate
questions.explanation: PSL ensures that credit flows to sectors that are often neglected by banks, including agriculture.

questions.question: With reference to Minimum Support Price (MSP), which of the following statements best describes its primary economic objective in the Indian agricultural context?
options: To ensure that the market price of crops never falls below the cost of production
To provide a price floor to protect farmers against sharp falls in agricultural prices
To eliminate the role of middlemen in the procurement of food grains
To ensure that agricultural produce is exported at competitive international prices
questions.explanation: MSP acts as a price floor to protect farmers from price volatility and ensure a minimum return on their investment.

questions.question: In the context of Indian agriculture, what is the most likely impact of an increase in the 'Terms of Trade' (ToT) for farmers?
options: Agricultural output will decrease due to higher input costs
Farmers' purchasing power increases as the price of farm produce rises relative to industrial goods
The government will be forced to reduce the Minimum Support Price
There will be a shift from food crops to cash crops to maintain equilibrium
questions.explanation: Improved Terms of Trade means the price of agricultural outputs increases faster than the price of agricultural inputs/industrial goods.

questions.question: Which of the following scenarios would most likely lead to 'Agricultural Distress' despite a bumper harvest in India?
options: An increase in the efficiency of the cold storage chain
A simultaneous surge in production and a lack of adequate procurement infrastructure
A decrease in the global demand for agricultural commodities
An increase in the allocation of credit through Kisan Credit Cards
questions.explanation: Overproduction without storage or procurement leads to a price crash, reducing farmers' income despite higher yields.

questions.question: Consider the impact of 'Price Deficiency Payment' schemes (like Bhavantar Bhugtan Yojana). How do they differ from traditional MSP procurement?
options: They require the government to physically procure and store the entire produce
They transfer the price difference to farmers without the government needing to procure the crop
They fix the market price regardless of the demand and supply dynamics
They are only applicable to non-food crops like cotton and jute
questions.explanation: Price deficiency payments pay the gap between market price and MSP without the logistical burden of physical procurement.

questions.question: Which of the following is a primary reason why 'Agricultural Marketing' in India is often characterized by 'Inefficiency'?
options: Lack of available land for cultivation
The prevalence of fragmented land holdings and a long chain of intermediaries
High usage of organic fertilizers across all crop types
The complete absence of government intervention in price discovery
questions.explanation: Fragmented holdings and multiple intermediaries increase transaction costs and reduce the farmer's share of the final consumer price.

questions.question: How does the 'Open-ended Procurement' policy of the Food Corporation of India (FCI) typically affect the market equilibrium?
options: It leads to a permanent shortage of food grains in the open market
It creates a surplus of stocks in government warehouses, often exceeding buffer norms
It forces private traders to enter the procurement market to compete with the government
It eliminates the need for the Public Distribution System (PDS)
questions.explanation: Open-ended procurement leads to the accumulation of stocks far beyond required buffer levels, causing storage losses.
